mnemonics: HILL Climb with Matrices: Remember that Hill Cipher uses matrices (the HILL) to encrypt and decrypt messages. Think of climbing a hill made of numbers and letters.
keyPoints: Hill Cipher is a polygraphic substitution cipher that encrypts blocks of letters (digraphs, trigraphs) simultaneously using linear algebra.
The core of the cipher is a square, invertible matrix (mod 26) known as the key matrix. The determinant of this matrix must be coprime to 26 for decryption to be possible.

Encryption is performed by multiplying the key matrix by a vector representing a block of plaintext letters. Decryption requires finding the inverse of the key matrix (mod 26) and performing a similar multiplication on the ciphertext.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check that the determinant of the key matrix is invertible modulo 26 (i.e., gcd(det(K), 26) = 1). If it is not, the matrix has no inverse and cannot be used for decryption.

Incorrectly handling the modulo 26 operation, especially with negative numbers. Results must always be mapped to a value between 0 and 25, representing the letters A-Z. For example, -1 mod 26 is 25, not -1.
